summary: Graham argues the 2004 exit polls were wrong because of a Bradley-effect-like bias: some Bush voters were less willing to disclose their vote to pollsters. He suggests a cultural gap between elite “NPR values” and ordinary voters led conservatives to be quieter, skewing nonresponse and imputation in exit polls.
key_points: Exit polls overestimated Kerry across swing states, implying systematic error rather than random noise.
Nonresponse in exit polls forces pollsters to impute votes, which fails if willingness to respond correlates with vote choice.
A cultural pressure to align with elite liberal norms may make conservative voters less forthcoming.
This dynamic would inflate reported support for the socially approved candidate and depress the other.
money_quotes: This works so long as there is no correlation between who people vote for and whether they're willing to talk about it.
Because people in the US are more conservative than they're willing to admit.
They know what they believe, but they also know what they're supposed to believe.
When the values of the elite are liberal, polls will tend to underestimate the conservativeness of ordinary voters.
No one who voted for Kerry did it as an act of quiet defiance.
key_insight: Exit polls can systematically miss the truth when social pressures make one side less willing to disclose their vote.
